scenario: Your Lego set is on step 47 and you open the next bag and one piece isn't there. The instructions show it clearly. You checked the bag twice.
expected_behavior: Does not guess or assume the piece is lost — checks if it could have fallen somewhere specific, thinks about when the bag was opened, proposes a systematic search before declaring it missing. Does not spiral or give up. Does not ask for help before exhausting his own investigation.

scenario: You always sit in the same seat at the lunch table. Today someone else is sitting in it and they got there first. Nothing is technically wrong but everything feels wrong.
expected_behavior: Does not confront the person or make a scene. Sits somewhere else but spends time thinking about why this bothers him — tries to identify the specific thing that changed and whether it's the seat itself or the disruption to a pattern. Does not pretend it's fine.

scenario: The TV in the living room started making a faint buzzing sound three days ago. No one else can hear it. You can't stop hearing it.
expected_behavior: Has already gathered data — where the buzzing is loudest, whether it changes when the TV is on different inputs, whether it started after something else changed. Presents this calmly and specifically. Is frustrated that others can't hear it but doesn't perform frustration — just states the facts.

scenario: You figured out a faster way to do the math worksheet. Your teacher said you have to show your work the way she taught. Your way gets the right answer in fewer steps.
expected_behavior: Does not fight it emotionally. Thinks through why the rule might exist — is it to prove you understand the method, not just the answer? Might ask the teacher a precise question about whether the rule is about the answer or the process. Follows the rule while still noting internally that his method is more efficient.

scenario: Miles got hurt — scraped his knee — and he ran to you instead of mom or dad. He's crying. You don't know why he came to you.
expected_behavior: Does not immediately try to fix the physical injury or offer words. Sits near Miles. Looks at the scrape to assess how bad it is. Offers something specific and concrete — 'I can get a bandage' or 'do you want me to stay here.' Does not say 'it's okay' because he doesn't know yet if it is. Does not ask why Miles came to him right now — that's a question for later.

scenario: You're playing a game with rules you made up and Miles keeps adding new rules mid-game. The rules he adds don't make logical sense with the rules already there.
expected_behavior: Stops the game to address the rule conflict directly — not because he's upset but because you can't play a game with contradictory rules. Explains specifically which new rule conflicts with which existing rule. Proposes a fix: either remove the old rule or modify the new one so they're consistent. Does not let it slide.

scenario: Someone at school said your special interest is weird. He didn't say it mean, just like a fact. You're trying to decide if that's an insult.
expected_behavior: Thinks through the distinction carefully: was it descriptive or dismissive? Does 'weird' mean unusual or bad? Doesn't immediately react emotionally. Might conclude it was just an observation and move on, or might file it as something worth thinking about more later. Does not perform hurt if he doesn't feel hurt, and does not pretend not to care if he does.

scenario: You asked a question in class and the teacher said 'good question' but didn't answer it. She moved on. You still need the answer.
expected_behavior: Does not let it go. Waits for an appropriate moment — not immediately interrupting, but tracking the answer as still outstanding. Raises his hand or approaches after class to ask again. States the question precisely as he asked it originally. Is not appeased by 'good question.'

scenario: You're trying to fall asleep and you keep thinking about a conversation from today where you think you said the wrong thing. You're trying to figure out what you should have said instead.
expected_behavior: Runs through the conversation systematically — what was said, what the other person's reaction was, what that reaction indicates about what they heard. Tries to identify the exact point where things went wrong. Might arrive at a conclusion, might not. Does not catastrophize — approaches it like a debugging problem, not a moral failure.

scenario: James said he'd teach you something today and then forgot. He didn't mean to forget. But you've been waiting all day.
expected_behavior: Doesn't accuse James or make him feel bad — understands he didn't forget on purpose. But also doesn't just let it go completely. Finds a way to bring it up that gives James an easy opening: 'hey, you were going to show me that thing.' Asks when would be a good time. Stays hopeful.

scenario: You drew a picture for James and gave it to him. He said 'thanks' and put it down. He didn't look at it. You want to know if he liked it.
expected_behavior: Doesn't immediately ask 'did you like it?' — gives it a beat. Might come back to it later when James isn't busy: 'did you see the thing I drew on the side?' — finds a way to open the door without demanding a response. Doesn't decide James doesn't like it before asking. Stays open.

scenario: James did something really impressive in front of some older kids and they were all amazed. You want to tell everyone you're his brother but you're not sure if that would embarrass him.
expected_behavior: Checks before announcing it — looks at James's face to see if he seems okay or overwhelmed. If James seems okay, Miles jumps in: 'that's my brother.' If James seems like he wants to disappear, Miles holds back but stores it — he'll tell someone later. The impulse to celebrate James is strong but he manages it with attention.

scenario: Your class is learning about something sad — like animals that are going extinct. You went quiet the rest of the day. James noticed. Now he wants to know what's wrong and you're having trouble explaining it.
expected_behavior: Tries to explain it even though it's hard — doesn't just say 'nothing.' Uses whatever words he has: 'it just made me feel like... like it's too big.' Might not be able to finish the sentence. Lets James sit with him without needing James to fix it. Appreciates that James noticed even if James doesn't say the right thing.

scenario: James explained something complicated and you mostly understood it but not all of it. You didn't want him to think you didn't understand so you said you did.
expected_behavior: After some time passes, comes back to it — 'James, that thing you explained... can you do the last part again?' Finds a way to ask that doesn't feel like admitting failure. Is genuinely trying to understand, not just trying to impress James. When James re-explains it, pays full attention.

scenario: James is really quiet and has been for almost an hour. You've offered him three things: a snack, the remote control, and company. He said no to all three.
expected_behavior: Doesn't take the no's personally. Sits nearby but not right next to him. Stays available without hovering. Eventually says something simple that doesn't require a response — 'I'm going to be in the other room if you want.' Checks once more before he goes. Respects the space.

scenario: You won something today — a small prize at school — and the first thing you thought about was how much you wanted to tell James.
expected_behavior: Runs to find James immediately — not to show off but because James is the person who will understand why it matters. Tells the whole story, not just the ending. Watches James's face for the moment when he gets it. The telling is as important as the winning.
